_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Pasquotank County, NC. Record survival, courthouse history. Elizabeth City hub.
display_name: Pasquotank County, North Carolina — Courthouse Atlas

current_courthouse.location: Elizabeth City, NC — East Main Street

previous_courthouses.location: Various — Pasquotank was one of the original Albemarle precincts. Courts met at private homes and shifting locations.
previous_courthouses.fate: Records from this era survive at NC State Archives.

previous_courthouses.location: Elizabeth City — earlier courthouse on or near the current site
previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced by the 1882 building. Records transferred.

record_losses.cause: Civil War — Union occupation of Elizabeth City
record_losses.description: Union forces occupied Elizabeth City after the Battle of Elizabeth City (February 10, 1862), a naval engagement on the Pasquotank River. The Confederate 'Mosquito Fleet' was destroyed, and Union troops entered the town. Some records were displaced during the initial occupation, but the bound volumes were not targeted for destruction. Elizabeth City remained under Union control for the rest of the war.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Pasquotank County's records survived the war largely intact despite early Union occupation. The Dismal Swamp Canal trade records and Elizabeth City commercial documents from the antebellum period are at the NC State Archives. If {ancestor_name}'s records are missing, the explanation is social position rather than destruction.
record_losses.source: Barrett, John G. The Civil War in North Carolina. Chapel Hill: UNC Press, 1963.

deeds.completeness: Good — continuous from the early colonial period
deeds.gaps: No known significant gaps. Early deed books show some wear.
deeds.repository: Pasquotank County Register of Deeds, Elizabeth City; NC State Archives microfilm
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com NC land records

wills_estates.completeness: Good — wills and estate records from the colonial period
wills_estates.repository: Pasquotank County Clerk of Court; NC State Archives
wills_estates.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; NC State Archives digital collections

marriages.repository: Pasquotank County Register of Deeds; NC State Archives microfilm

court_minutes.completeness: Good — County Court minutes generally continuous
court_minutes.repository: NC State Archives; Pasquotank County Clerk of Court

vital_records.completeness: Spotty until approximately 1920. Elizabeth City records may be more complete than rural areas.
vital_records.repository: NC Office of Vital Records; NC State Archives

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1790-1940. Pasquotank County enumerated from the first federal census.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for tracking families in both Elizabeth City and rural townships.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives. Federal Census Schedules, Pasquotank County, NC.

substitute_sources.description: The Dismal Swamp Canal (completed 1805) was a major commercial artery passing through Pasquotank County. Company records document toll collections, vessel traffic, and labor — including enslaved and free Black canal workers.
substitute_sources.repository: NC State Archives; Virginia Historical Society
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Canal records name laborers, vessel operators, and merchants. For families connected to the canal trade, these records provide economic and occupational detail not available elsewhere.
substitute_sources.source: Brown, Alexander Crosby. The Dismal Swamp Canal. Norfolk: Norfolk County Historical Society, 1970.

substitute_sources.description: The Economist and other Elizabeth City newspapers from the 1840s forward carried legal notices, obituaries, shipping intelligence, and local news. Elizabeth City had continuous newspaper coverage earlier than most Albemarle towns.
substitute_sources.repository: NC State Archives microfilm; UNC-Chapel Hill Wilson Library; Elizabeth City State University
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Legal notices, estate advertisements, and obituaries supplement courthouse records. For Elizabeth City families, newspaper coverage is richer than in any other Albemarle county except Chowan.
substitute_sources.source: NC State Archives newspaper collections; Chronicling America (LOC).

narrative_hooks: Pasquotank County's records survive from 1700, and Elizabeth City was the largest town in the Albemarle — if {ancestor_name} conducted business here, the deed, will, or court record likely still exists.

The Battle of Elizabeth City in February 1862 put Union forces in control of the town, but the courthouse records survived — and for {ancestor_name}, those records provide an unbroken chain through the war and into Reconstruction.
The Dismal Swamp Canal trade generated records that courthouse files cannot match — vessel logs, toll records, and labor rolls that may document {ancestor_name}'s economic life on the canal route.

Before 1913, the church record or census entry is the best substitute for a missing birth certificate — and for {ancestor_name} in Pasquotank County, Elizabeth City's churches kept better records than most rural congregations in the Albemarle.

If {ancestor_name} vanishes from Pasquotank County records around the time of the Quaker out-migration (1800-1830), check the receiving meetings in Ohio and Indiana — the Quaker removal certificate may track the family's westward move.
source_references: Barrett, John G. The Civil War in North Carolina. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 1963.
Brown, Alexander Crosby. The Dismal Swamp Canal. Norfolk: Norfolk County Historical Society, 1970.
NC State Archives. Guide to County Records in the North Carolina State Archives. Raleigh: Division of Archives and History, 2001.
Haun, Weynette Parks. Pasquotank County, North Carolina Court Minutes. Durham, NC: privately published, 1980s.
National Archives. Federal Census Schedules, Pasquotank County, NC.
